title: Slab and coil combined serving bowl
subtitle: A wide air-dry clay bowl with a slab base and coil-built walls
excerpt: A wide serving bowl with a rolled slab base and coiled walls. The slab provides a stable, flat base and the coils allow the walls to curve outward gradually. The finished bowl is sealed for display use. Air-dry clay. No kiln or wheel required.
sourceNotes: Combined slab and coil hand-building technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A rope of clay rolled between the palms or on a board, used to build up walls by stacking.
Scratching the surface and applying liquid clay before joining two pieces to ensure a strong bond.
Liquid clay mixed with water to a thick cream consistency, used as a clay adhesive when joining pieces.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to attach new pieces.

content.text: Combining a slab base with coil walls is one of the most versatile hand-building approaches. The slab provides a flat, even base that is harder to achieve by coiling alone. The coiled walls allow you to curve and flare the form outward gradually as each ring is added.
Rolling the base

Roll a slab 6 mm thick and cut a circle 150 mm in diameter. This is the bowl base. Allow to stiffen for 20-30 minutes until it holds its shape without sagging, but is still damp enough to accept new clay.
Building the coil walls
Roll a
coil
about 12 mm thick and long enough to go around the base circumference. Use
score and slip

on the outer edge of the base and the bottom of the coil. Press the coil onto the base edge, joining the ends. Blend the inside join with a fingertip or small wooden tool: press the clay downward and inward in a circular motion to fuse the coil to the base. Blend the outside as well.
Allow the first coil to stiffen to
leather-hard

before adding the next. This prevents the wall from collapsing under the weight of new clay. Add each successive coil slightly outside the one below to gradually flare the bowl shape outward. A serving bowl should flare from 150 mm at the base to about 230-250 mm at the rim over 5-6 coil rings, each ring 10-12 mm tall.
Smoothing the walls
Once all coils are in place and firmly joined, smooth the inside walls with a wet sponge or wooden rib in long strokes from base to rim. The
slip
that forms on the surface during smoothing helps fill any small cracks. The outside can be left with coil texture visible, or smoothed to a uniform surface.
Drying, sealing, and use

Dry slowly for 3-5 days. Air-dry clay bowls in large forms crack easily if dried in a warm room: cover loosely with plastic and allow to dry at room temperature. Once bone-dry, sand the rim and outside with fine sandpaper. Seal with two coats of diluted PVA or spray acrylic sealant. Air-dry clay bowls should not hold water directly. Use them for fruit, bread, dry goods, or as display vessels.
Variations
Press a decorative texture into the outside walls before the final coil dries.
Pinch the rim into gentle waves by pressing the top coil inward at regular intervals while still soft.
Build a tall version using 10-12 coils with the walls kept vertical for a storage crock rather than a bowl.

rows.cause: New coils were added before the previous coil reached leather-hard, or score and slip was skipped
rows.fix: Fill cracks with fresh slip when dry. Dampen the repair slightly before applying.

rows.cause: Base slab was too thin or was moved before it stiffened
rows.fix: Dry the base on a flat board without moving it. A plaster bat will pull moisture out evenly and prevent warping.

rows.problem: Outside walls look uneven and lumpy
rows.fix: Roll each coil against a ruler or check against a consistent guide before applying. Smooth the outside with a damp sponge to blend.
